title: Employment Tribunals Act 1996
chapters: 48 sections in 3 Parts (Part I — Employment Tribunals, Part II — Employment Appeal Tribunal, Part III — Supplementary), supplemented by the Employment Tribunal Procedure Rules 2024 (SI 2024/1155, in force 6 January 2025)

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Industrial Tribunals Act 1996 (renamed by Employment Rights (Dispute Resolution) Act 1998)
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — consolidation of industrial tribunal legislation
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Originally enacted as the Industrial Tribunals Act 1996, a consolidation statute. Renamed to Employment Tribunals Act 1996 by the Employment Rights (Dispute Resolution) Act 1998 s.1. Significantly amended by: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (ACAS early conciliation, financial penalties), Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 (online procedure). The Employment Tribunal Procedure Rules 2024 (SI 2024/1155) replaced the 2013 Rules with effect from 6 January 2025.

significantAmendments.effect: Renamed industrial tribunals to employment tribunals. All references in all legislation updated.

Inserted s.18A (mandatory ACAS early conciliation before proceedings). Inserted s.12A (financial penalties for aggravating features). Amended s.18 (conciliation provisions). Created the stop-the-clock time limit extension mechanism (ERA 1996 s.207B).

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Tribunals and Employment Appeal Tribunal Fees Order 2013 (SI 2013/1893)
significantAmendments.effect: Introduced fees for issuing claims and for hearings. DECLARED UNLAWFUL by the Supreme Court in R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] UKSC 51. Fees abolished and refunded. The ETA itself was not amended — the fees Order was quashed.

Replaced the Employment Tribunals (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations 2013. New rules in force from 6 January 2025. Modernised procedure including digital filing, case management, and costs provisions.

precedingDocuments.title: Industrial Tribunals Act 1996 (original enactment)
precedingDocuments.significance: The ETA 1996 is a consolidation statute — it brought together provisions previously scattered across multiple Acts including the Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978 and the Trade Union Reform and Employment Rights Act 1993.

precedingDocuments.title: Donovan Report — Royal Commission on Trade Unions and Employers' Associations (1968)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Donovan Commission recommended the establishment of industrial tribunals as the primary forum for resolving individual employment disputes. The modern tribunal system descends directly from this recommendation.

precedingDocuments.title: R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] UKSC 51
precedingDocuments.significance: The Supreme Court declared the employment tribunal fees regime unlawful as an unjustified impediment to access to justice. This landmark ruling restored fee-free access to employment tribunals and is foundational to understanding the current procedural framework.

leadingCases.name: R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor
leadingCases.principle: Employment tribunal fees (£390-£1,200 per claim) were declared unlawful as they effectively prevented access to justice. The right of access to courts and tribunals is a constitutional right that cannot be abrogated by subordinate legislation without express parliamentary authority.
leadingCases.heldText: The fees Order was unlawful under both domestic law (as an unjustified interference with the constitutional right of access to justice) and EU law (as it rendered the enforcement of EU employment rights excessively difficult or virtually impossible).
leadingCases.significance: LANDMARK — the most significant UK Supreme Court judgment on employment tribunal procedure. Restored fee-free access to tribunals. Established that the constitutional right of access to justice cannot be undermined by fees that render claims practically impossible to bring. All fees paid since 2013 were refunded.

leadingCases.name: Dedman v British Building and Engineering Appliances Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 'not reasonably practicable' test for extending the three-month time limit: the question is whether it was reasonably feasible for the claimant to present the claim in time. Ignorance of rights may be a relevant factor but is not automatically sufficient.
leadingCases.heldText: The test is whether it was reasonably feasible, not merely physically possible, for the claimant to present the complaint within the time limit. If there was a reasonable explanation for the delay, the tribunal may extend time.
leadingCases.significance: Foundational — established the 'not reasonably practicable' test that governs time limit extensions for unfair dismissal and most ERA 1996 claims. Employers defending late claims must engage with this test.

leadingCases.name: Palmer v Southend-on-Sea Borough Council
leadingCases.principle: The 'not reasonably practicable' test means 'not reasonably feasible' — a liberal construction of the words, falling between 'physically possible' and 'reasonable'. The test is stricter than the 'just and equitable' discretion available for discrimination claims.
leadingCases.heldText: 'Reasonably practicable' means more than merely what is reasonably capable physically of being done. It means something like 'reasonably feasible.'
leadingCases.significance: Definitional — the Court of Appeal's gloss on 'not reasonably practicable' that tribunals apply to every late unfair dismissal claim.

leadingCases.name: Robertson v Bexley Community Centre
leadingCases.principle: The 'just and equitable' time limit extension for discrimination claims (Equality Act 2010 s.123) gives tribunals a broader discretion than the 'not reasonably practicable' test. However, the discretion is not unfettered — tribunals must weigh the prejudice to both parties and have regard to all relevant circumstances.
leadingCases.heldText: There is no presumption in favour of extending time. The exercise of discretion is the exception rather than the rule. The onus is on the claimant to convince the tribunal that it is just and equitable to extend time.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employer respondents — establishes that even under the more generous 'just and equitable' test, time limits are not merely aspirational. Employers can and should challenge late discrimination claims.

leadingCases.name: Luton Borough Council v Haque
leadingCases.principle: The ACAS early conciliation time limit extension under ERA 1996 s.207B operates cumulatively, not alternatively — subsections (3) and (4) both apply where relevant, one after the other.
leadingCases.heldText: Where it applies, subsection 207B(4) operates to extend the time limit as first modified by subsection (3). Subsection (3) applies in every case; the application of subsection (4) is conditional.
leadingCases.significance: Essential for calculating time limits with ACAS early conciliation — the 'stop the clock' and 'one month minimum' extensions are cumulative. Employers and their advisers must calculate both extensions to determine the true deadline.

leadingCases.name: Science Warehouse Ltd v Mills
leadingCases.principle: The ACAS early conciliation requirement under s.18A is satisfied where the EC certificate relates to a 'matter' rather than a specific 'claim'. A certificate obtained before the events giving rise to a claim can satisfy s.18A if the matter is sufficiently connected.
leadingCases.heldText: Section 18A will be satisfied where there is an EC Certificate in respect of a 'matter' rather than the more specific 'claim'. The tribunal should adopt a permissive approach.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates the tribunal's permissive approach to early conciliation — beneficial for claimants but important for employer respondents to understand: a pre-dismissal EC certificate may cover a subsequent unfair dismissal claim.

parts.title: Part 1 — ACAS Early Conciliation: The Mandatory Gateway

sections.title: Requirement to contact ACAS before instituting proceedings — mandatory early conciliation
sections.summary: Before a prospective claimant can present a claim to an employment tribunal, they MUST first contact ACAS and provide prescribed information to enable ACAS to explore conciliation. ACAS assigns a conciliation officer who has a prescribed period (initially one month, extendable by two weeks) to attempt to settle the dispute. If settlement fails, ACAS issues an Early Conciliation Certificate with a unique reference number. The claim form (ET1) cannot be accepted by the tribunal without this certificate number. Limited exemptions exist for multiple claimant claims, interim relief applications, and national security cases.
sections.text:
(1) Before a person ('the prospective claimant') presents an application to institute relevant proceedings relating to any matter, the prospective claimant must provide to ACAS prescribed information, in the prescribed manner, about that matter.

(2) On receiving the prescribed information in the prescribed manner, ACAS shall send a copy of it to a conciliation officer.

(3) The conciliation officer shall, during the prescribed period, endeavour to promote a settlement between the persons who would be parties to the proceedings.

(4) If—
(a) during the prescribed period the conciliation officer concludes that a settlement is not possible, or
(b) the prescribed period expires without a settlement having been reached,
the conciliation officer shall issue a certificate to that effect, in the prescribed manner, to the prospective claimant.

(8) A person who is subject to the requirement in subsection (1) may not present an application to institute relevant proceedings without a certificate under subsection (4).
sections.keyPrinciple: ACAS early conciliation is MANDATORY — a claim submitted without an EC certificate number will be rejected by the tribunal. This is not optional advice-seeking; it is a statutory gateway. The conciliation period also 'stops the clock' on time limits under ERA 1996 s.207B.
refs: ERA 1996 s.207B (stop the clock — time limit extension for early conciliation)
ERRA 2013 s.7 (inserted s.18A into the ETA 1996)
Early Conciliation: Exemptions and Rules of Procedure Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/254)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 13 (rejection of claim without EC number)
Luton Borough Council v Haque [2018] UKEAT (cumulative time extensions)
Science Warehouse v Mills [2015] UKEAT ('matter' vs 'claim')

leadingCases.name: Luton Borough Council v Haque
leadingCases.principle: The ACAS early conciliation time extensions under s.207B(3) and (4) are cumulative, not alternative. Both apply where relevant.
leadingCases.heldText: Sub-section 207B(4) operates to extend the time limit as first modified by sub-section (3). Sub-section (3) applies in every case; the application of sub-section (4) is conditional.
leadingCases.significance: Essential for time limit calculations — employers must compute both extensions to determine the true deadline.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Early conciliation explained
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guide to the early conciliation process including how to notify, what happens during conciliation, and how the EC certificate works

commonErrors: Assuming early conciliation is optional — it is mandatory. A claim submitted without an EC certificate number will be rejected
Failing to calculate time limit extensions correctly with early conciliation — the 'stop the clock' (s.207B(3)) and 'one month minimum' (s.207B(4)) provisions are cumulative per Luton v Haque
Assuming an EC certificate obtained before dismissal cannot cover a subsequent unfair dismissal claim — per Science Warehouse v Mills, the certificate covers a 'matter' not a specific 'claim'
Communications with the ACAS conciliation officer being treated as admissible — s.18(7) makes anything communicated to a conciliation officer inadmissible in evidence (with limited exceptions)
Ignoring ACAS early conciliation contact from an employee — this is a formal pre-action step and should trigger the employer's internal dispute response process

versions.text: Inserted by ERRA 2013 s.7(1). Originally applied from 6 May 2014 (statutory instruments phased commencement). The prescribed conciliation period was initially one month from Day A, extendable by two weeks with mutual agreement.
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government introduced mandatory early conciliation as part of its employment law reform programme, aiming to reduce the number of claims reaching tribunal by facilitating early settlement. ACAS reports consistently show that approximately 60-70% of early conciliation notifications result in either settlement or the claim not proceeding.

sections.title: Time limit extension for ACAS early conciliation — the 'stop the clock' provision
sections.summary: ERA 1996 s.207B provides the mechanism by which the time limit for presenting an employment tribunal claim is extended to account for the period spent in ACAS early conciliation. Two extensions operate cumulatively: (1) the period from Day A (contact with ACAS) to Day B (receipt of EC certificate) is not counted in calculating the time limit ('stop the clock'), AND (2) if the time limit would otherwise expire within one month of Day B, it is extended to one month after Day B ('minimum one month extension'). This calculation is complex and errors are common — both employers and claimants frequently miscalculate the deadline.
sections.text:
ERA 1996 s.207B (Extension of time limits to facilitate conciliation before institution of proceedings):

(3) In working out when a time limit set by a relevant provision expires the period beginning with the day after Day A and ending with Day B is not to be counted.

(4) If a time limit set by a relevant provision would (if not extended by this subsection) expire during the period beginning with Day A and ending one month after Day B, the time limit expires instead at the end of that period.

Where:
- Day A = the day the prospective claimant contacts ACAS under s.18A(1)
- Day B = the day the prospective claimant receives (or is treated as receiving) the EC certificate under s.18A(4)
sections.keyPrinciple: Both extensions are CUMULATIVE, not alternative (Luton v Haque). The 'stop the clock' applies in every case; the 'minimum one month' applies only if needed. An out-of-time claim cannot be saved by late early conciliation — s.207B only extends time for claimants who contacted ACAS WITHIN their original time limit.
refs: ETA 1996 s.18A (mandatory early conciliation requirement)
ERA 1996 s.111(2) (three-month time limit for unfair dismissal)
Equality Act 2010 s.123(1) (three-month time limit for discrimination — 'just and equitable' extension)
Luton Borough Council v Haque [2018] UKEAT (cumulative operation confirmed)
commonErrors: Treating subsections (3) and (4) as alternative extensions — they are CUMULATIVE. Both apply where relevant

Assuming early conciliation can rescue an already out-of-time claim — s.207B only extends time for claimants who contacted ACAS within their original time limit. A claim that was already time-barred before ACAS was contacted remains time-barred

Miscounting Day A and Day B — Day A is the day ACAS was contacted (not the day the dispute arose). Day B is the day the EC certificate was received (or deemed received). Email certificates are deemed received on the day sent; postal certificates on the day of ordinary delivery

Failing to check the EC certificate date when responding to a claim — the time limit calculation depends on the exact dates on the certificate. An employer defending a late claim should always request and scrutinise the EC certificate

parts.title: Part 2 — Time Limits: The Three-Month Gateway

sections.title: Employment tribunal time limits — the complete framework
sections.summary: Most employment tribunal claims must be presented within three months of the act complained of. The standard time limit is three months minus one day (e.g., act on 1 May → time limit expires at 11:59pm on 31 July). Different claims have different extension tests: unfair dismissal and most ERA 1996 claims use the strict 'not reasonably practicable' test (Dedman v British Building); discrimination claims under EqA 2010 use the broader 'just and equitable' test (Robertson v Bexley Community Centre); redundancy payment claims have a six-month time limit. Going through a grievance or appeal procedure does NOT stop the clock — the employee must still present their claim within the time limit (or contact ACAS within the time limit to benefit from the s.207B extension).
sections.text:
Standard time limits:

(1) Unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.111): 3 months from the effective date of termination. Extension: 'not reasonably practicable' test.

(2) Unlawful deductions from wages (ERA 1996 s.23): 3 months from the date of the deduction (or last in a series). Extension: 'not reasonably practicable'.

(3) Discrimination (EqA 2010 s.123): 3 months from the date of the act complained of (or last act in a continuing course of conduct). Extension: 'just and equitable' (broader discretion).

(4) Redundancy payments (ERA 1996 s.164): 6 months from the relevant date. Extension: 'just and equitable'.

(5) Equal pay (EqA 2010 s.129): 6 months from end of employment or end of stable working relationship.

(6) Automatically unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.108(3)): 3 months — same as ordinary unfair dismissal.

(7) Whistleblowing detriment (ERA 1996 s.48): 3 months from the date of the act. Extension: 'not reasonably practicable'.

(8) Interim relief (ERA 1996 s.128): 7 days from the effective date of termination. ACAS early conciliation NOT required for interim relief.

All time limits except interim relief are subject to the ACAS early conciliation 'stop the clock' extension under ERA 1996 s.207B.
sections.keyPrinciple: Time limits are strictly enforced in employment tribunals. An employer receiving a claim should ALWAYS check whether it was presented in time — and whether the ACAS early conciliation dates are correct. A successful time limit challenge means the claim is dismissed without a hearing on the merits.
refs: ERA 1996 s.111 (unfair dismissal — 3 months)
ERA 1996 s.23 (deductions — 3 months)
EqA 2010 s.123 (discrimination — 3 months, 'just and equitable' extension)
ERA 1996 s.164 (redundancy — 6 months)
ERA 1996 s.128 (interim relief — 7 days, no EC required)
ERA 1996 s.207B (ACAS early conciliation time extension)
Dedman v British Building [1974] ICR 53 ('not reasonably practicable' test)
Palmer v Southend-on-Sea BC [1984] ICR 372 ('reasonably feasible')
Robertson v Bexley Community Centre [2003] EWCA Civ 576 ('just and equitable' discretion)

leadingCases.name: Dedman v British Building and Engineering Appliances Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 'not reasonably practicable' test means whether it was reasonably feasible for the claimant to present the claim in time. Ignorance of rights may be relevant but is not automatically sufficient.
leadingCases.heldText: Was it reasonably feasible for the complaint to have been presented within the prescribed period? If the answer is no, was it then presented within a reasonable period?
leadingCases.significance: Foundational — establishes the test that governs every late unfair dismissal and deductions claim.

leadingCases.name: Robertson v Bexley Community Centre
leadingCases.principle: The 'just and equitable' extension for discrimination claims gives tribunals a broader discretion, but there is no presumption in favour of extension. The onus is on the claimant.
leadingCases.heldText: There is no presumption that a tribunal should exercise its discretion to extend time. The exercise of the discretion is the exception rather than the rule.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employer respondents challenging late discrimination claims — the 'just and equitable' test is broader but not unlimited.

commonErrors: Assuming a pending internal grievance or appeal procedure extends the time limit — it does NOT. The employee must contact ACAS within the original time limit regardless of internal procedures
Failing to check whether a claim was presented in time — a successful time limit challenge is a complete defence. Employers should always check dates and scrutinise the EC certificate

Confusing the two extension tests — 'not reasonably practicable' (unfair dismissal, deductions) is STRICT; 'just and equitable' (discrimination) is BROADER. Different claims in the same proceedings may have different extension standards

Miscalculating the three-month period — the period runs from the date of the act complained of. Three months minus one day. For dismissal: from the effective date of termination, NOT the date of the dismissal meeting

sections.title: Financial penalties for aggravating features
sections.summary: Gives employment tribunals the power to impose a financial penalty on a respondent employer where a breach of a worker's employment rights has one or more 'aggravating features'. The penalty is payable to the Secretary of State (not to the claimant). For financial awards: the penalty is 50% of the award, minimum £100, maximum £20,000. For non-financial awards (e.g., reinstatement): minimum £100, maximum £5,000. The penalty is halved if paid within 21 days. The tribunal must have regard to the employer's ability to pay.
sections.text:
12A Financial penalties

(1) Where an employment tribunal determining a claim involving an employer and a worker—
(a) concludes that the employer has breached any of the worker's rights to which the claim relates, and
(b) is of the opinion that the breach has one or more aggravating features,
the tribunal may order the employer to pay a penalty to the Secretary of State (whether or not it also makes any other order on the claim).

(2) The tribunal must have regard to the employer's ability to pay when deciding whether to order the employer to pay a penalty under this section and (subject to subsections (3) to (7)) in deciding the amount of the penalty.
sections.keyPrinciple: This is a PUNITIVE penalty paid to the Government — on top of any compensation paid to the claimant. The aggravating features test captures deliberate breaches, malicious conduct, and repeated violations by experienced employers. Factors include: deliberate or malicious action, employer having a dedicated HR team (should have known better), and repeat breaches of the same right.
refs: ERRA 2013 s.16 (inserted s.12A into ETA 1996)
s.12A(9)-(10) (50% reduction if paid within 21 days)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 73 (costs orders — separate power)
ERA 1996 s.38 (additional award for failure to provide s.1 statement — separate)
commonErrors: Assuming financial penalties are rare — the tribunal has discretion to impose them in ANY case where the breach has aggravating features. Deliberate wage underpayment, systematic failure to follow statutory dismissal procedures, and repeated breach of the same right are all candidates

Failing to recognise that the penalty is SEPARATE from compensation — the claimant receives compensation; the penalty goes to the Government. The total cost to the employer is compensation PLUS penalty
Not taking advantage of the 21-day early payment discount — paying the penalty within 21 days halves it

versions.policyRationale: Coalition Government introduced financial penalties as a deterrent against employers who deliberately or repeatedly breach employment rights.

versions.text: Maximum penalty increased to £20,000 by SI 2019/224.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Tribunals (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) (Amendment) Regulations 2019
versions.policyRationale: Government quadrupled the maximum penalty to provide a stronger deterrent. The £5,000 maximum was considered insufficient to deter larger employers.

parts.title: Part 4 — Costs, Enforcement, and Penalties for Non-Payment

sections.title: Costs and expenses — when tribunals order costs against a party
sections.summary: Employment tribunals can order one party to pay the other party's costs. Unlike civil courts, costs do not automatically follow the event — the losing party does not automatically pay the winner's costs. Costs orders are made only in specific circumstances: where a party (or representative) has acted vexatiously, abusively, disruptively, or otherwise unreasonably, or where the claim or response had no reasonable prospect of success. The tribunal may also make a wasted costs order against a representative personally. Preparation time orders are available for unrepresented parties. The maximum costs order without a detailed assessment is £20,000.
sections.text:
Costs orders (ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rules 73-84):

(1) A costs order may be made where a party or representative has acted vexatiously, abusively, disruptively, or otherwise unreasonably in either the bringing or conducting of the proceedings.

(2) A costs order may be made where the claim or response had no reasonable prospect of success.

(3) A costs order may be made where a hearing has been postponed or adjourned on the application of a party.

(4) Maximum without detailed assessment: £20,000.

(5) No maximum with detailed assessment.

(6) Wasted costs orders may be made against representatives personally.

(7) The tribunal may have regard to the paying party's ability to pay.

(8) Deposit orders: the tribunal may order a party to pay a deposit of up to £1,000 as a condition of continuing a specific allegation or argument that has little reasonable prospect of success.
sections.keyPrinciple: Costs in tribunals are the exception, not the rule. However, employers who pursue a defence with no reasonable prospect of success, or who conduct proceedings unreasonably (late disclosure, failure to comply with orders, vexatious behaviour), risk a costs order. Equally, employers can seek costs against claimants who bring vexatious or hopeless claims.
refs: ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rules 73-84 (full costs regime)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 38 (deposit orders)
Barnsley Metropolitan Borough Council v Yerrakalva [2011] EWCA Civ 1255 (costs — unreasonable conduct)
Vaughan v London Borough of Lewisham [2013] UKEAT (wasted costs against representatives)

leadingCases.name: Barnsley Metropolitan Borough Council v Yerrakalva
leadingCases.principle: In considering costs for unreasonable conduct, the tribunal should look at the whole picture — the nature, gravity, and effect of the unreasonable conduct as a matter of common sense assessment. There is no requirement for a precise causal link between the unreasonable conduct and specific costs incurred.
leadingCases.heldText: The vital point in exercising the discretion to order costs is to look at the whole picture of what happened in the case and to ask whether there has been unreasonable conduct by the claimant in bringing and conducting the case.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority for employer respondents seeking costs — the 'whole picture' approach means that a pattern of unreasonable conduct can support a costs order even if no single incident is extreme.

commonErrors: Assuming the loser pays costs in an employment tribunal — costs do not automatically follow the event. A successful claimant does not automatically recover legal fees; a successful respondent does not automatically recover its costs

Failing to seek a costs order when the claimant has conducted proceedings unreasonably — employers should actively consider costs applications where the claimant has been vexatious, failed to comply with orders, or pursued hopeless claims
Ignoring deposit order opportunities — a deposit order of up to £1,000 at a preliminary hearing can deter weak claims and, if the claim fails, the deposit is paid to the respondent as costs
Not recording unreasonable conduct during proceedings — if a costs application may be made at the end, evidence of unreasonable conduct should be documented contemporaneously

sections.title: Enforcement of tribunal awards and penalties for non-payment
sections.summary: Employment tribunal awards are enforceable through the county court (or Sheriff Court in Scotland) as if they were county court judgments. From April 2016, the Government introduced a separate penalty regime for employers who fail to pay tribunal awards or ACAS-conciliated settlement sums: enforcement officers can issue financial penalty notices of 50% of the unpaid amount (minimum £100, maximum £25,000). The penalty is halved if paid within 14 days. This regime was introduced because research showed that approximately one-third of successful claimants were not receiving their awards.
sections.text:
Enforcement:

(1) Awards may be enforced as if they were county court orders (s.15, County Courts Act 1984).

(2) The claimant can apply for enforcement through the county court using standard enforcement methods: attachment of earnings, charging orders, third party debt orders, or warrant of control.

Penalty for non-payment (s.37A-37F, inserted by Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015):

(3) Where an employer fails to pay a tribunal award or ACAS-conciliated settlement sum, an enforcement officer may issue a warning notice.

(4) If the employer still fails to pay, the enforcement officer may issue a financial penalty notice.

(5) The penalty is 50% of the unpaid amount, subject to a minimum of £100 and a maximum of £25,000.

(6) The penalty is halved if paid within 14 days.

(7) Penalties are payable to the Secretary of State.

(8) The employer may appeal to the tribunal against the imposition of the penalty.
sections.keyPrinciple: Failing to pay a tribunal award is not just a civil enforcement matter — it triggers a SEPARATE government penalty of up to £25,000. The total cost of non-payment is: the original award + interest + enforcement costs + up to 50% penalty. Non-payment is never the rational economic choice.
refs: ETA 1996 s.37A-37F (penalty for non-payment)
Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015 s.150 (inserted penalty provisions)
County Courts Act 1984 s.85 (enforcement of tribunal awards as county court judgments)
ETA 1996 s.14 (interest on tribunal awards — currently 8% p.a.)
commonErrors: Ignoring a tribunal award in the hope it will not be enforced — approximately one-third of awards historically went unpaid, which led to the introduction of the penalty regime. The enforcement landscape has tightened significantly
Failing to pay interest on a tribunal award — interest accrues at 8% per annum from the date of the award (ETA s.14)

Not recognising that the penalty for non-payment applies equally to ACAS-conciliated settlements (COT3 agreements) — failure to pay a COT3 sum triggers the same penalty regime as failure to pay a tribunal award

sections.title: ACAS Code of Practice uplift — 25% increase or decrease in compensation
sections.summary: Where an employment tribunal finds that a claim involves a matter to which a relevant ACAS Code of Practice applies, and either the employer or the employee has unreasonably failed to comply with the Code, the tribunal may increase or decrease the award by up to 25%. This applies to the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (the most commonly applied Code). An employer who follows the ACAS Code can argue for a 25% reduction in the claimant's award; an employer who unreasonably fails to follow it faces a 25% uplift. This is one of the most significant financial incentives in the tribunal system.
sections.text:
ERA 1996 s.207A (Effect of failure to comply with Code: adjustment of awards):

(1) This section applies to proceedings before an employment tribunal relating to a claim by an employee under any of the jurisdictions listed in Schedule A2.

(2) If, in the case of proceedings to which this section applies, it appears to the employment tribunal that—
(a) the claim to which the proceedings relate concerns a matter to which a relevant Code of Practice applies,
(b) the employer has failed to comply with that Code in relation to that matter, and
(c) that failure was unreasonable,
the employment tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances to do so, increase any award it makes to the employee by no more than 25%.

(3) If, in the case of proceedings to which this section applies, it appears to the employment tribunal that—
(a) the claim to which the proceedings relate concerns a matter to which a relevant Code of Practice applies,
(b) the employee has failed to comply with that Code in relation to that matter, and
(c) that failure was unreasonable,
the employment tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances to do so, decrease any award it makes to the employee by no more than 25%.
sections.keyPrinciple: The 25% uplift/reduction is a MAJOR financial incentive for employers to follow the ACAS Code. In a £50,000 discrimination award, a 25% uplift adds £12,500. In a £30,000 unfair dismissal award where the claimant failed to use the grievance procedure, a 25% reduction saves the employer £7,500. Following the ACAS Code is not just good practice — it directly affects the bottom line.
refs: ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (No. 1, 2015)
ERA 1996 Schedule A2 (jurisdictions to which s.207A applies — includes unfair dismissal, discrimination, deductions, whistleblowing)
ACAS Code of Practice on Flexible Working (No. 5, 2024) — s.207A does NOT apply to the flexible working Code
Tips Act 2023 Code of Practice — s.207A does NOT apply to the Tips Code (separate enforcement)
Lawless v Print Plus [2010] UKEAT (25% is the maximum — the tribunal has discretion on the percentage)
commonErrors: Failing to follow the ACAS Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures — this can add 25% to any tribunal award. The Code requires: investigation, written notification of allegations, a disciplinary hearing with the right to be accompanied, and a right of appeal

Not seeking a reduction when the claimant failed to raise a grievance — if the employee unreasonably failed to use the grievance procedure before bringing a claim, the employer should argue for up to 25% reduction

Assuming the 25% uplift applies to all ACAS Codes — it applies ONLY to Codes listed in Schedule A2. The Flexible Working Code (No. 5, 2024) and the Tips Code are NOT covered by the s.207A adjustment mechanism
Applying the uplift to the basic award as well as the compensatory award — the uplift applies to the compensatory award only, not the basic award (which is a statutory formula)

sections.title: Settlement agreements and COT3 — the only lawful routes to settle employment claims
sections.summary: ERA 1996 s.203 provides that statutory employment rights cannot be waived EXCEPT through two specific mechanisms: (1) an ACAS-conciliated settlement (COT3 agreement) under ETA 1996 s.18-19A, or (2) a valid settlement agreement meeting the statutory conditions in ERA 1996 s.203(3)-(4). Any other purported waiver of employment rights — including in a contract of employment, side letter, or informal agreement — is VOID. A settlement agreement must: be in writing, relate to specific complaints, state that the statutory conditions are met, and the employee must have received independent legal advice from a qualified adviser.
sections.text:
ERA 1996 s.203(1): Any provision in an agreement (whether a contract of employment or not) is void in so far as it purports—
(a) to exclude or limit the operation of any provision of this Act, or
(b) to preclude a person from bringing any proceedings under this Act before an employment tribunal.

s.203(2): Subsection (1) does not apply to any agreement to refrain from instituting or continuing proceedings where—
(a) a conciliation officer has taken action under section 18 of the Employment Tribunals Act 1996, or
(b) the conditions regulating settlement agreements under this Act are satisfied in relation to the agreement.

s.203(3)-(4): A settlement agreement is valid only if:
(a) it is in writing,
(b) it relates to the particular complaint or proceedings,
(c) the employee has received advice from an independent adviser (solicitor, certified trade union official, or advice centre worker),
(d) the adviser has a current policy of professional indemnity insurance,
(e) the agreement identifies the adviser, and
(f) the agreement states that the conditions in s.203 are satisfied.
sections.keyPrinciple: There are ONLY two lawful ways to settle employment claims: a COT3 (through ACAS) or a settlement agreement (with independent legal advice). Everything else is void. An employer who gets an employee to sign a waiver without independent legal advice has NO enforceable settlement — the employee can still bring tribunal proceedings.
refs: ERA 1996 s.203 (restrictions on contracting out)
ETA 1996 s.18 (ACAS conciliation — COT3 agreements)
ETA 1996 s.19A (conciliation after proceedings instituted)
EqA 2010 s.144 (equivalent provision for discrimination claims)
ACAS Code of Practice on Settlement Agreements (No. 4, 2013)
Sutherland v Network Appliance Ltd [2001] IRLR 12 (settlement agreement requirements strict)
commonErrors: Getting an employee to sign a waiver of claims without ensuring they have received independent legal advice — the agreement is VOID without it and the employee can still bring proceedings
Assuming a compromise clause in the contract of employment prevents tribunal claims — s.203(1) renders any such clause void
Failing to specify the particular complaint(s) being settled — a settlement agreement must relate to specific identified complaints, not just 'all claims'

Not paying the employee's reasonable legal fees for taking advice on the settlement agreement — while not strictly required by statute, it is standard practice and ACAS guidance recommends it. Refusing to pay creates a practical barrier that may undermine the agreement

Confusing COT3 agreements with settlement agreements — a COT3 is brokered by ACAS and does NOT require independent legal advice. A settlement agreement is a direct agreement between employer and employee that DOES require independent legal advice. Both are valid but the requirements are different

parts.title: Part 5 — Procedure, Hearings, and the Employment Appeal Tribunal

sections.title: Tribunal procedure — hearings, case management, and the 2024 Rules
sections.summary: Employment tribunal proceedings are governed by the Employment Tribunal Procedure Rules 2024 (SI 2024/1155), which replaced the 2013 Rules with effect from 6 January 2025. Key procedural features: claims are presented on form ET1 (with EC certificate number), responses on form ET3 (within 28 days of receiving the claim), preliminary hearings for case management and strike-out applications, final hearings with oral evidence and cross-examination. The tribunal comprises a judge (and, in some cases, two lay members). Hearings are generally in public. Witness statements are exchanged before the hearing.
sections.text:
Key procedural provisions:

(1) Claims: presented on form ET1, online or by post. Must include EC certificate number. Tribunal staff refer claims for judicial sifting (Rule 13).

(2) Response: form ET3 must be presented within 28 days of receiving the claim. Late responses require an application for extension (Rule 18). If no response is submitted, the tribunal may issue a default judgment (Rule 22).

(3) Preliminary hearings (Rule 54): case management (listing, disclosure, witness orders) and/or determination of preliminary issues (time limits, employment status, strike-out applications).

(4) Strike-out (Rule 37): the tribunal may strike out a claim or response if it has no reasonable prospect of success, has been conducted scandalously/unreasonably/vexatiously, there has been non-compliance with an order, the claim has not been actively pursued, or a fair hearing is no longer possible.

(5) Final hearing: evidence-in-chief (usually by witness statement taken as read), cross-examination, submissions. Decision with written reasons.

(6) Reconsideration (Rule 69): a party may apply for reconsideration of a judgment within 14 days on the ground that it is necessary in the interests of justice.
sections.keyPrinciple: The 28-day deadline for submitting the ET3 response is critical — an employer who misses this deadline may face a default judgment. The new 2024 Rules modernise digital filing and case management. Employers should respond promptly, engage with case management, and prepare witness evidence thoroughly.
refs: ET Procedure Rules 2024 (SI 2024/1155) — full rules in force 6 January 2025
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 13 (initial sifting)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 18 (ET3 response — 28 days)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 37 (strike-out)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 54 (preliminary hearings)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 69 (reconsideration — 14 days)
Presidential Guidance on case management
commonErrors: Missing the 28-day deadline for the ET3 response — this can result in a default judgment against the employer. The clock starts from receipt of the claim, not from the date of issue
Failing to apply for a deposit order or strike-out at a preliminary hearing when the claim has no reasonable prospect of success — these are powerful procedural tools for respondent employers
Not preparing witness statements to the required standard — statements should deal with all relevant facts and be exchanged by the deadline ordered by the tribunal

Assuming tribunal proceedings are like civil court proceedings — tribunals are designed to be more accessible and informal. There is no formal pleading requirement and the overriding objective is to deal with cases fairly and justly

Not requesting written reasons for a tribunal decision — under s.7 and Rule 60, a party can request written reasons within 14 days of the decision being sent. Written reasons are essential for any appeal and for understanding the tribunal's legal reasoning

Failing to comply with an 'unless order' (Rule 38) — an unless order specifies that if a party fails to comply with a case management order by a deadline, their claim or response will be struck out automatically. Non-compliance is self-executing — there is no further hearing before strike-out takes effect

Not applying for a witness order (Rule 30) where a critical witness is reluctant — the tribunal can compel attendance of witnesses by issuing an order. This is particularly important for employer respondents who need evidence from former employees or third parties

Misunderstanding tribunal composition — most claims are now heard by a judge sitting alone (unfair dismissal, deductions, breach of contract). Discrimination claims may be heard by a panel of three (judge + two lay members). The composition can affect how the tribunal weighs evidence and credibility

sections.title: Employment Appeal Tribunal — jurisdiction, procedure, and further appeals
sections.summary: The Employment Appeal Tribunal (EAT) hears appeals from employment tribunal decisions on points of law only. There is no right of appeal on the facts — the EAT will not substitute its own view of the evidence for that of the tribunal. An appeal must be lodged within 42 days of the date on which the written reasons were sent to the parties (EAT Practice Direction). From the EAT, further appeals lie to the Court of Appeal (with permission) and then to the Supreme Court. The EAT sits with a High Court judge (or circuit judge) and, in certain cases, two lay members with specialist knowledge.
sections.text:
Part II of the Employment Tribunals Act 1996:

s.21: Jurisdiction — the EAT has jurisdiction to hear appeals from employment tribunal decisions on any question of law arising from the proceedings.

s.28: Appeals from the EAT to the Court of Appeal (England and Wales) or Court of Session (Scotland), with permission.

s.35: The EAT may not hear an appeal unless it was brought within the applicable time limit (42 days from written reasons).

Points of law include: misapplication of a legal test, perversity (where the decision is one that no reasonable tribunal could have reached), procedural irregularity, failure to give adequate reasons, and errors in the application of statutory provisions.
sections.keyPrinciple: The EAT hears appeals on points of LAW only — not on the facts. An employer who disagrees with the tribunal's factual findings cannot appeal merely because a different conclusion was possible. To succeed on appeal, the employer must identify a specific legal error.
refs: ETA 1996 s.21 (EAT jurisdiction — questions of law)
ETA 1996 s.28 (appeal to Court of Appeal)
EAT Practice Direction 2023 (42-day time limit for appeals)
Yeboah v Crofton [2002] EWCA Civ 794 (perversity — extremely high threshold)

leadingCases.name: Yeboah v Crofton
leadingCases.principle: An appeal on the ground of perversity will succeed only where the decision is one that 'no reasonable tribunal could have reached' or is 'an overwhelming case'. The threshold is extremely high.
leadingCases.heldText: Such appeals ought only to succeed where an overwhelming case is made out that the employment tribunal reached a decision which no reasonable tribunal, on a proper appreciation of the evidence and the law, would have reached.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the extremely high bar for perversity appeals — employers cannot use the EAT as a second bite at the factual cherry.

commonErrors: Attempting to appeal factual findings to the EAT — the EAT does not rehear evidence or substitute its own factual conclusions. Only errors of LAW are appealable
Missing the 42-day time limit for lodging an EAT appeal — this is strictly enforced. The clock runs from the date written reasons were SENT, not received

Confusing the grounds for appeal — disagreement with the tribunal's decision is not a ground. The employer must identify a specific legal error: wrong test applied, relevant law ignored, procedural irregularity, or perversity (overwhelmingly high threshold)

parts.title: Part 6 — Publicity Restrictions and Access to Justice

sections.title: Publicity restrictions, open justice, and the constitutional right of access
sections.summary: Tribunal hearings are generally in public (open justice principle). Restricted reporting orders can be made in cases involving allegations of sexual misconduct (s.11) or cases where evidence of a personal nature is likely to be heard in disability discrimination claims (s.12). These orders prohibit publication of information likely to identify the affected person. The constitutional right of access to employment tribunals was definitively affirmed by the Supreme Court in R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] UKSC 51, which struck down the employment tribunal fees regime as an unlawful impediment to access to justice.
sections.text:
Open justice:
(1) Employment tribunal hearings are generally in public (ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 48).
(2) Judgments and written reasons are published on the GOV.UK tribunal decisions database.

Restricted reporting orders:
(1) s.11: the tribunal may make a restricted reporting order in cases involving allegations of sexual misconduct, prohibiting publication of information likely to identify the person making or affected by the allegation until the promulgation of the decision.

(2) s.12: the tribunal may make a restricted reporting order in disability discrimination cases where evidence of a personal nature is likely to be heard.

Access to justice:
(1) R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] UKSC 51: employment tribunal fees (£390-£1,200) declared unlawful as an unjustified impediment to the constitutional right of access to justice. Fees abolished and refunded.
(2) Employment tribunals are now fee-free for claimants to bring claims.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employment tribunal proceedings are public. Judgments are published. Restricted reporting orders are available in sexual misconduct and disability cases but are the exception. Access to employment tribunals is fee-free following the UNISON decision — this is a constitutional protection, not merely a policy choice.
refs: ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 48 (hearings in public)
ET Procedure Rules 2024 Rule 49 (private hearings — limited circumstances)
ETA 1996 s.11 (sexual misconduct — restricted reporting)
ETA 1996 s.12 (disability — restricted reporting)
R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] UKSC 51 (fees unlawful)
Scott v Scott [1913] AC 417 (open justice principle)
commonErrors: Assuming tribunal proceedings are confidential — they are NOT. Hearings are in public and judgments are published. Employers should prepare for the possibility of press attendance and published findings

Seeking anonymity in tribunal proceedings without a statutory basis — restricted reporting orders are only available in sexual misconduct and disability cases. There is no general right to anonymity for employers or respondents
Assuming that settlement agreements can prevent publication of a tribunal judgment — settlement must occur before judgment. Once a judgment is issued, it is a public document

pendingAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — Fair Work Agency enforcement
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 establishes the Fair Work Agency which will consolidate enforcement of employment tribunal awards alongside other employment rights enforcement. When commenced, this may change the enforcement landscape for unpaid tribunal awards from passive county court enforcement to proactive state enforcement.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — potential shift from passive to active enforcement of tribunal awards

pendingAmendments.instrument: Employment Tribunal Procedure Rules 2024 — early implementation
pendingAmendments.status: The new 2024 Procedure Rules (SI 2024/1155) replaced the 2013 Rules from 6 January 2025. The first year of operation will reveal any practical issues or judicial interpretive guidance on the new rules.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — new procedural requirements for all parties

pendingAmendments.instrument: Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 — outstanding provisions
pendingAmendments.status: Several provisions of the JRCA 2022 affecting the ETA 1996 (including online procedure and EAT digital filing) have not yet been commenced. Monitor commencement orders.
